root_text.sn36.5:1.3: Sukhā vedanā, dukkhā vedanā, adukkhamasukhā vedanā.
root_text.sn36.5:1.4: Sukhā, bhikkhave, vedanā dukkhato daṭṭhabbā, dukkhā vedanā sallato daṭṭhabbā, adukkhamasukhā vedanā aniccato daṭṭhabbā.
root_text.sn36.5:1.5: Yato kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhuno sukhā vedanā dukkhato diṭṭhā hoti, dukkhā vedanā sallato diṭṭhā hoti, adukkhamasukhā vedanā aniccato diṭṭhā hoti—
root_text.sn36.5:1.6: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ‘bhikkhu sammaddaso acchecchi taṇhaṁ, vivattayi saṁyojanaṁ, sammā mānābhisamayā antamakāsi dukkhassā’ti.

translation_text.sn36.5:1.4: Pleasant feeling should be seen as suffering. Painful feeling should be seen as a dart. Neutral feeling should be seen as impermanent.
translation_text.sn36.5:1.5: When a mendicant has seen these three feelings in this way,
translation_text.sn36.5:1.6: they’re called a mendicant who sees rightly, has cut off craving, cast off the fetters, and by rightly comprehending conceit has made an end of suffering.
translation_text.sn36.5:2.1: A mendicant who sees pleasure as pain,
translation_text.sn36.5:3.4: they’re without defilements in this very life.
translation_text.sn36.5:3.5: That knowledge master is firm in principle;
translation_text.sn36.5:3.6: when their body breaks up, they can’t be reckoned.”
